title: “Functional Analysis of Darwin Sea Level Pressure (1951-2025)” author: “Sanjib Samadder | Data Scientist” output: pdf_document: toc: true toc_depth: 2 latex_engine: xelatex html_document: toc: true toc_depth: 2 df_print: paged

Loading Dataset and Initial Setup

library(fda)
data <- as.matrix(read.table("data/fda-SLP.dat"))

Task 1 :Implement Functional Smoothing Techniques.

Setting up data structure & smoothing parameter

darwin_slp_by_month = t(data) # Transpose to 12 months × 75 years

timeRng = c(1, 12)
time = seq(1, 12, 1)
nbasis = 12
Tempbasis = create.fourier.basis(timeRng, nbasis)
harmaccelLfd = vec2Lfd(c(0, (2*pi/diff(timeRng))^2, 0), timeRng)

# Determine smoothing parameter λ by GCV
loglam = seq(-2, 5, 1)
nlam = length(loglam)
dfsave = rep(NA, nlam)
names(dfsave) = loglam
gcvsave = dfsave
invisible({
  for (ilam in 1:nlam) {
    lambda = 10^loglam[ilam]
    fdParobj = fdPar(Tempbasis, harmaccelLfd, lambda)
    smoothlist = smooth.basis(time, darwin_slp_by_month, fdParobj)
    dfsave[ilam] = smoothlist$df
    gcvsave[ilam] = sum(smoothlist$gcv)
  }
})

Setting up the plots and choosing best \(\lambda\) vlaue for propepr fit

par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 2))

# GCV for smoothing parameter selection
plot(loglam, gcvsave, type='b', lwd=2, 
     ylab=expression(GCV(lambda)),
     xlab=expression(log[10](lambda)),
     main="GCV for Smoothing Parameter Selection")
abline(v=0, col="red", lty=2)

# Smoothing with λ = 1 (10^0) as GCV graph shows global minimum value at 0 
lambda = 10^0
fdParobj = fdPar(Tempbasis, harmaccelLfd, lambda)
darwin_slp_fit = smooth.basis(time, darwin_slp_by_month, fdParobj)
darwin_slp_fd = darwin_slp_fit$fd
fdnames = list("Month (1=January)", "Darwin SLP (mb relative to 1000 mb)", "Year")
darwin_slp_fd$fdnames = fdnames

plotfit.fd(darwin_slp_by_month[,1], time, darwin_slp_fd[1], lwd=2, 
           main = expression(paste("GCV Choice : Smoothing with ", lambda, " = 1")),
           xlab = "Month", ylab = "SLP (mb relative to 1000 mb)")

# My choice : Smoothing with λ = 0.1 (10^-1) as 10^0 was bit underfit 
lambda = 10^-1
fdParobj = fdPar(Tempbasis, harmaccelLfd, lambda)
darwin_slp_fit = smooth.basis(time, darwin_slp_by_month, fdParobj)
darwin_slp_fd = darwin_slp_fit$fd
fdnames = list("Month (1=January)", "Darwin SLP (mb relative to 1000 mb)", "Year")
darwin_slp_fd$fdnames = fdnames

plotfit.fd(darwin_slp_by_month[,1], time, darwin_slp_fd[1], lwd=2,
           main = expression(paste("My choice : Smoothing with ", lambda, " = 10^-1")),
           xlab = "Month", ylab = "SLP (mb relative to 1000 mb)")

#All 75 smoothed curves
invisible(plot(darwin_slp_fd, lwd=0.8, col=rainbow(75), 
     xlab="Month", ylab="SLP (mb relative to 1000 mb)",
     main="Smoothed all 75 Years (1951-2025)"))

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Explanation of my chosen \(\lambda\)

Task 2: Functional Descriptive Statistics

Task 2(i): Elementary pointwise mean and standard deviation of Darwin SLP

mean_slp = mean.fd(darwin_slp_fd) # calculating mean 
stddev_slp = std.fd(darwin_slp_fd) # creating standard deviation function

# Creating plots
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
invisible(plot(mean_slp, lwd=2, xlab="Month", ylab="Pressure (mb relative to 1000 mb)", 
     main="Pressure Mean"))
invisible(plot(stddev_slp, lwd=2, xlab="Month", ylab="Pressure (mb)", 
     main="Pressure \nStandard Deviation"))

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Justification of my Mean and Standard Deviation

Mean

  • From the mean curve, we can see in Jan to April pressure is low around 7/8 mb
  • Highest pressure is in July to August around 12/13 mb
  • This seasonal pattern reflects the influence of the Australian monsoon and the migration of the South Pacific High

Standard Deviation

  • The standard deviation function reveals interannual variability
  • Higher variability in summer (Australia summer is Aug to Feb) - around 1 mb where in winter (Jun - Sep) low around 0.75
  • This indicates that ENSO-related pressure anomalies are strongest during the Southern Hemisphere summer

Task 2(ii): Bivariate Covariance Function

slp_covariance = var.fd(darwin_slp_fd) #bivariate covariance
slp_covariance_mat = eval.bifd(time, time, slp_covariance) #Evaluate covariance matrix at monthly points

# 3D perspective plot of covariance surface
persp(time, time, slp_covariance_mat,
      theta=-75, phi=20, r=7, expand=0.5,
      ticktype='detailed',
      xlab="Month", ylab="Month",
      zlab="Covariance (mb²)", col=rainbow(5000), 
      main="Bivariate Covariance - Darwin SLP")

Justification : Bivariate Covariance Function

Task 2(iii): Contour plot of the variance surface

# Contour plot of covariance matrix
contour(time, time, slp_covariance_mat, 
        xlab="Month", ylab="Month", lwd=2, labcex=1, 
        main="Contour Plot - Covariance Surface")

Justification : Contour plot

deviation function.

Task 2(iv): Phase-Plane plots

# Phase-plane plot for the mean function
phaseplanePlot(time, mean_slp,
               labels=list(evalarg=time, labels=1:12),
               xlab='Pressure Velocity (mb/month)', 
               ylab='Pressure Acceleration (mb/month²)', 
               main="Phase-Plane Plot - Darwin SLP Mean")

Justification : Phase-Planeplot

Task 3: Functional Principal Component Analysis (FPCA)

Task 3(i): Scree plot to determine number of principal components

# Preset number of PCs to evaluate
nharm = 3
darwin_pca_temp = pca.fd(darwin_slp_fd, nharm)

# Extract eigenvalues
fdaeig = darwin_pca_temp$values
neig = 6

# Fit linear trend to later eigenvalues
x = matrix(1, neig - nharm, 2)
x[,2] = (nharm + 1):neig
y = as.matrix(fdaeig[(nharm + 1):neig])
c = lsfit(x, y, int = FALSE)$coef

# Scree plot
plot(1:neig, fdaeig[1:neig], "b", 
     xlab = "Principal Component Number", 
     ylab = "Eigenvalue", 
     main = "Scree Plot - Darwin SLP")
lines(1:neig, c[1] + c[2] * (1:neig), lty = 2, col = "red")
abline(v = 2.5, col = "blue", lty = 2)

Justification: Scree plot

Task 3(ii): Calculate eigenvalues and variance proportions

# Select 2 principal components based on scree plot
nharm = 2
darwin_pca = pca.fd(darwin_slp_fd, nharm)

# Display eigenvalues
print(darwin_pca$values[1:2])
## [1] 4.553020 2.251256
cat(paste("PC1:", round(darwin_pca$varprop[1] * 100, 2), "%\n"))
## PC1: 53.34 %
cat(paste("PC2:", round(darwin_pca$varprop[2] * 100, 2), "%\n"))
## PC2: 26.37 %
# Plot both principal components in same horizontal line
par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(1, 2, 3, 2))
plot.pca.fd(darwin_pca, expand = 1.5)

par(ask = FALSE)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Justification : Eigenvalues and variance proportions

  • The two main components are presented as a perturbation from the mean, where the mean is represented by the solid line.From the R code, we can see that the first two eigenvalues are 4.55 and 2.25, respectively.
  • The first two components explain 53.34% and 26.37% of the total variation, respectively.Collectively, these first two components can explain up to 79.71% of the total variation.
  • The figure above represents the unrotated functional principal components. The first functional principal component indicates a constant shift in the mean, which represents the overall pressure anomalies in all months. Negative values indicate low pressure, or El Niño conditions, whereas positive values indicate high pressure, or La Niña conditions.
  • The second functional principal component indicates the contrast between summer and winter months. It represents the timing or strength of the annual cycle in the pressure anomalies.

Task 3(iii): VARIMAX rotation technique with eigenvalues

Plots are on the next page

# Apply VARIMAX rotation
darwin_pca_rotated = varmx.pca.fd(darwin_pca)

# Plot rotated principal components
par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(1, 2, 3, 2))
plot.pca.fd(darwin_pca_rotated, expand = 1.5)

par(ask = FALSE)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1)) 
# Display rotated eigenvalues
print(darwin_pca_rotated$values[1:2])
## [1] 4.291875 2.512954
# Display rotated variance proportions
print(darwin_pca_rotated$varprop)
## [1] 0.5027230 0.2943516

Description : VARIMAX rotation

  • The two rotated principal component functions are given below. These are plotted as a function of perturbation from the mean function, which is given by the solid line.

  • From the results obtained using R, we find that the first two eigenvalues are 4.29 and 2.51, respectively. This is not surprising because these two principal components explain 79.71 percent of the variation. Then we have the plot of the rotated functional principal components. The first component explains 50.27 percent of the variation. This component is associated with the summer ENSO signal. Negative values indicate low pressure systems (El Niño-like), while positive values indicate high pressure systems (La Niña-like) occurring in the austral summer season. On the other hand, the second component explains 29.44 percent of the variation. This component is associated with the winter season. This is because this component is associated with the Australian monsoon and winter pressure systems.

  • The VARIMAX rotation is used to simplify the principal component structure. This is because this rotation separates the summer ENSO signal and winter seasonality.
